title: A Propitiation Before the Father
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: A Propitiation Before the Father - 1 John 2_1-2 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Do Not Sin: The Basic Exhortation
subpoints: John says simply: my little children, I write these things to you that you may not sin
The New Testament is not shy about commanding Christians not to sin — this is not legalism but plain Christianity
Legalism occurs only when one counts on obedience to the law as the means of acceptance before God; pursuing obedience because God has redeemed and transformed you is ordinary Christianity
The end goal of grace is obedience: Titus 2:11-12 — the grace of God teaches us to deny ungodliness and to live soberly, righteously, and godly

outline.title: The Pastoral Tenderness of John's Exhortation
subpoints: John addresses the whole congregation as my little children — not just the young people but everyone
His tenderness comes across as he pleads with them to live holy lives rather than commanding them with cold authority
These things refers back to 1 John 1:6-10: do not walk in darkness, walk in the light, do not deny your sin, confess, do not claim sinless perfection
The path to overcoming sin is simply following the Lord Jesus Christ by his grace and relying on him for ability to obey

outline.title: When We Sin: We Have an Advocate
subpoints: Even as we pursue holiness we will fall at times, and John does not want the saints to fall into despair and cycle deeper into sin
We have an advocate with the Father — one who speaks for us, represents us, wants the court to declare us innocent
He is named: Jesus Christ the righteous — his righteous standing is the basis on which he can plead for us before the Father
John uses the same word for the Spirit's ministry as advocate (John 14-16) and for Christ's here, picturing twin advocacy: Christ before the Father, Spirit before us

outline.title: He Himself Is the Propitiation
subpoints: Propitiation: something God provides in his grace to bring man into his presence with the assurance that they are accepted, since he has removed the barrier that kept them at a distance
It is Christ himself who is the propitiation — not just the priest who offers, but the offering itself and even the mercy seat on which it is poured
1 John 4:10: in this is love, not that we loved God but that He loved us and sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins — the Father appointed this out of love, not reluctance
Romans 3:22-26: God set forth Christ as the propitiation by his blood to demonstrate his righteousness — justifying the one who has faith in Jesus

outline.title: Not for Our Sins Only, But for the Whole World
subpoints: This does not refer to a universal offer of salvation or universal forgiveness of all people's sins
Rather it speaks to the uniqueness of Christ's propitiation: he is the only propitiation for anyone in the world
Anyone who wishes to come to the Father must come through this one and only propitiation — there is no other path of access
Freedom from despair: knowing we have an advocate who has already paid and is actively interceding frees us to obey without paralyzing fear of failure

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro preaches an evening sermon from 1 John 2:1-2, a passage that balances the call to holiness with the comfort of Christ's ongoing advocacy. He opens with a famous Bob Newhart comedy sketch in which a therapist's entire therapeutic method is two words — Stop it — to illustrate the point that at a basic level, the solution for sin in the Christian life is simple: do not sin. But he carefully distinguishes simple from easy, and addresses the objections of those who believe telling Christians not to sin is either naively impossible or illegitimately legalistic.

The sermon's first movement defends the legitimacy of commanding Christians to obey. Grace is given precisely so that we can obey (Titus 2:11-12); election is unto obedience (1 Peter 1); and the end goal of the gospel is a people zealous for good works. Legalism is not the same as serious obedience — it only occurs when one trusts in obedience as the grounds of divine acceptance. The apostle John's pastoral tenderness in addressing his congregation as his little children further shows that the call to holiness is delivered with love, not harsh demand.

The heart of the sermon treats the nature of Christ's advocacy and propitiation. Christ stands before the Father as the righteous one — his righteousness is the basis on which he can plead for sinners. He is not pleading against the Father's anger but was sent by the Father out of love to be the propitiation (1 John 4:10). The term propitiation connects to the mercy seat in the most holy place: Christ is not only the high priest who brings the blood, he is the blood, and he is the mercy seat on which the blood is poured. Romans 3:22-26 is read as showing that God himself justifies the one who has faith in Jesus, looking at the Son and seeing the believer in him.

The sermon closes with a liberating application: obedience looks the same for every category of person — single, married, young, old. It is faith in Christ, trusting that he stands before the Father for you, resulting in hard work to obey, and when you fail, turning to Christ to be forgiven and continuing. The knowledge that you have an advocate frees you to risk for Christ without paralyzing fear, because even when you fail, he forgives.
transcript:
Let us turn in our Bibles if you can, remaining standing for a moment longer, and turn to 1 John chapter 2. I am going to read 2 verses of 1 John chapter 2, verses 1 and 2. 1 John chapter 2 verse 1. This is the Word of our Lord.

*My little children, these things I write to you, so that you may not sin. And if anyone sins, we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. And He Himself is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the whole world.*

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father, we pray that You would open our eyes to see wonderful things concerning You from this passage. We pray in Jesus name. Amen. Please be seated.

On February 11th, 1995, Bob Newhart hosted a Saturday Night Live episode. You know, the infamous or famous — have you seen it? Show on NBC. I refer to this skit several times but it really fits what we are going to be considering here tonight. Part of that episode was a skit in which Newhart plays a therapist. And his client comes in to talk with him because she has this life-dominating fear of being buried alive in a box. Part of the dialogue goes like this. Newhart plays the part of Dr. Slicer and the client's name is Katherine. They talk with one another this way.

*Dr. Slicer: Tell me about the problem that you wish to address.*
*Katherine: Oh okay, well, I have this fear of being buried alive in a box. I just start thinking about being buried alive and I begin to panic.*
*Dr. Slicer: Has anyone ever tried to bury you alive in a box?*
*Katherine: No, no, but truly thinking about it does make my life horrible. I mean, I cannot go through tunnels or being in an elevator or in a house. Anything boxy.*
*Dr. Slicer: So what you're saying is you are claustrophobic.*
*Katherine: Yes, yes, that's it.*
*Dr. Slicer: All right, well let's go, Katherine. I'm going to say two words to you right now. I want you to listen to them very, very carefully. Then I want you to take them out of the office with you and incorporate them into your life.*
*Katherine: Shall I write them down?*
*Dr. Slicer: No. Well, if it makes you comfortable. It's just two words so we find that most people can remember them.*
*Katherine: Okay.*
*Dr. Slicer: Are you ready?*
*Katherine: Yes.*
*Dr. Slicer: Okay, here they are: Stop it.*
*Katherine: Stop it?*
*Dr. Slicer: Stop it. Yes. S-T-O-P, new word, I-T. Stop it.*
*Katherine: So what are you saying?*
*Dr. Slicer: You know, it's funny. I say two simple words and I cannot tell you the amount of people who say exactly the same thing you are saying.*

It is a really funny skit — unless you fear being buried alive in a box, in which case it is not so funny. But it is funny because deep down it makes sense. It makes sense to us. We prefer complicating things and do not like when the answer to our problems is simpler than we expected. I think that is a natural human tendency. We tend to want to complicate things instead of just taking things at face value and seeing that the solution may be simple.

And I want to understand that the solution to a problem being simple is not the same as being easy. There is a difference between simplicity and easiness. Now often we hide behind the it is complicated phrase in order not to do what God calls us to do. At a very basic level the solution for sin in the life of the Christian is simple. The solution is not sinning. As Dr. Swatren would say, the solution is stopping it. And when the Christian sins, the solution is also simple: he or she turns to Christ for forgiveness and strength to not sin again.

And that is the point that the Apostle John makes in these two verses here in 1 John chapter 2. And that is really the outline for the sermon. First we are going to look at the fact that we are not to sin. And secondly we are going to see that even when we sin we do not have to despair — we turn to Christ for forgiveness and strength to follow the Lord.

## Do Not Sin (verse 1a)

So verse 1, John says, my little children, these things I write to you so that you may not sin. So John says, do not sin. Stop it. Yes, that is how John starts here. And notice that the Apostle was not afraid to tell Christians not to sin.

We developed this idea that we are bound to sin, that we cannot just say do not sin, because that is what we are and we are just going to do it. But that is not how the New Testament as a whole or the Scriptures as a whole portray the Christian. It has become unpopular in some Reformed circles to preach on commands or to tell people what to do from the pulpit or in counseling. One objection comes from the camp that says that we must emphasize only the grace of God, and to tell people to do things is legalism. So we should not really be telling people what to do. Another objection comes from the camp that says that preaching should only reflect what Christ has done — only the indicative of the Scriptures, only the things that talk about Christ, should be the only content of preaching and the Holy Spirit should be the one that applies the imperative, the commands of the Bible.

I even heard a guy say once that the height of arrogance is for the preacher to try to apply the Word of God because that is the job of the Holy Spirit. The problem is that if you preach the Bible, you come across these pesky commandments. These things that we are supposed to tell people to do. When God chose to reveal himself to the people, the primary thing he did was give us ten things — ten things that we should actually do and not do. The Bible itself often tells people what to do. And if we are going to preach and believe what the Bible says, we are going to preach and believe in obedience to the law of God.

We have to stop thinking that we are bound to sin. That is a very defeated position. It will actually lead to despair. F.F. Bruce, a British commentator, says, sin indeed is so thoroughly unpractical of the Christian life that a life which is marked by sin cannot be called Christian. We have just accepted the fact that Christians are just those people who sin, which is not how the Bible describes those who follow the Lord Jesus Christ.

And people say that there is something contradictory between the grace of God and also preaching the law of God. And yet the grace of God is always given to us so that we can obey. The end goal of grace is obedience. In Titus chapter 2 verse 11, Paul is talking to Titus about the grace of God and he says, for the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared to all men — a great statement about the grace of God. The grace of God has appeared. And then he continues in verse 12 that this grace is teaching us, doing something: teaching us that denying ungodliness and worldly lusts we should live soberly, righteously and godly in the present age. So what is it that the grace of God teaches those of us who follow Jesus Christ? It teaches how to live a holy life. How to deny self. How to live for the glory of God.

In 1 Peter chapter 1 we are told that we are elected unto what? Unto obedience. The result of God, the Triune God of the Bible, before time began choosing a people to himself so that they would be delivered from sin unto righteousness, with the goal of obedience.

People often issue the claim of legalism. Legalism in the church tends to have the same force as the claim of racism in the broader society. If somebody wants to dismiss you, what do they do? They call you a racist, no matter what. Legalism in the church has the same idea — if you want to stop someone's argument, you just call it legalism. But really, legalism only takes place when one is counting on his obedience to some sort of standard as the means through which the Father will receive him into his fellowship. Seeking to obey God because God has worked in you, transformed you, given you the Spirit, redeemed you in Jesus Christ — is not legalism. It is just plain old Christianity. It is plain old the religion of the Scriptures.

Now notice how pastoral John is when he speaks to them. He says in verse 1, my little children. He tells them not to sin but he does it as a loving father, not like Bob Newhart. He is not just saying stop it. He addresses the whole congregation as my little children — not just the children but everyone in the congregation. And his tenderness comes across in the plea to the congregation to live holy lives. He pleads with them: do not sin, live a holy life. And he is writing this epistle for the very purpose of exhorting the people of God and us through them to live holy lives that are marked by obedience to the Word of God.

He says in verse 1: my little children, these things I write to you so that you may not sin. Now these things I believe refer back to what he said already in 1 John 1:6-10, all those if statements that we looked at two weeks ago. So what are the things that he has written that will help us to live a holy life? Well, in verse 6 of chapter 1 it says, do not walk in darkness. In verse 7, walk in the light — faith in Christ in obedience to his Word. In verse 8, do not deny that you sin. In verse 9 it says confess your sins so that you can be forgiven. In verse 10, do not deny that you are capable of sinning. And the consequences of following what John says: you are going to have fellowship with the Father and with one another. The truth is going to be in your hearts and lives. You are cleansed by the blood of Jesus Christ. You are going to have forgiveness and the Word of God will dwell in you.

So he says, I write these things that you may not sin. Simply following the Lord Jesus Christ by his grace, relying on him and nothing else in order to be able to succeed in obeying God. Because at the core of obedience is the atoning work of Christ. You cannot obey apart from that.

## When We Sin: Do Not Despair (verses 1b-2)

So John says, do not sin. But if you do, look at the rest of verse 1 and verse 2: if anyone sins, we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.

This is the reality: even as we attempt to live holy lives, we will fall at times. And as we grow in Christ that should be happening less and less, though we will never attain perfection in this life — that is what we are pressing forward toward according to Paul in Philippians chapter 3. But we fall, we stumble, we struggle at times. And John does not want his little children to fall into despair and go deeper into sin. We have a tendency, when we fail and when we fall, to allow that to drag us further into despair. And as we get into despair because of our sins, we tend to sin more. John does not want us to go there. He wants us to know that in our pursuit of God, if we fail at times, we do not have to go into despair. We simply turn to Christ to be forgiven.

He reminds us of something he had already mentioned in 1:7 — that the blood of Christ cleanses us from all our sins. Notice that he says in verse 1, if anyone sins, we have an Advocate with the Father. The anyone refers to those who are in the we group in the same verse. Jesus is the Advocate of those who are in the we group — that is, those who are united to one another by faith in Jesus Christ who is our head, which is part of the fellowship we saw in verse 1 of chapter 1. He is the Advocate of his church.

Now, the term advocate refers to a person who acts as a spokesperson or representative of someone else's position, purpose, or cause, especially before a judge in a court of law. An advocate is one who speaks for you, who intercedes for you, who wants the best for you, who wants the court to declare you to be innocent of all charges. And it is definitely a word that the Apostle John likes, since he is the only one who uses it in the whole New Testament. He uses it four times in the Gospel — each time referring to the Holy Spirit, and our Bibles tend to translate it as helper or counselor — and then he uses this one time here in 1 John chapter 2 for the Lord Jesus Christ.

So the Spirit and Jesus have this twin ministry in which Jesus advocates for us before the Father and the Spirit advocates for the Father and the Son before us. The difference is that the Spirit is telling us who Christ and the Father are. But the Son has something to bring before the Father, and he brings his own blood.

Notice what John says in verse 1: we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. He could have stopped after saying we have an Advocate with the Father — it is understood, it is Jesus Christ. But he names our Advocate: Jesus Christ, the righteous. He can stand before the Father on our behalf because he is righteous. Do you realize — and I hope you do — that all our righteousness, all our rightness, all our doing things for good, whatever that good is, amount to dirty, the dirtiest, most disgusting sort of rags? But Jesus is righteous and is completely acceptable to the Father.

To be really graphic: in Brazil, the sewer system cannot handle toilet paper. So after you use the toilet paper, you put it in the garbage. And then the garbage has to take it out. To come before the Father saying, God look at me, look at how great I am — is to grab that garbage full of used toilet paper and say, God You should accept me because I bring You this gift. That is silly. That is plain old foolish. But the Son comes in his own righteousness before the Father — true righteousness — and he pleads for us. And the Father hears him.

You know, Christ pleads for us through his own blood. In verse 2, John says, and He Himself is the propitiation for our sins. He is our propitiation. And that is another word that we are not used to seeing very often. So what is propitiation? F.F. Bruce says, propitiation is something which God has provided in his grace to bring man into his presence with the assurance that they are accepted by him, since he has removed the barrier that kept them at a distance. That is what Christ does. He removes the barrier that has kept us at a distance. He brings us and the Father together. And he is doing that for you right now. If you believe in Jesus, he is right now interceding for you. If you sin, he is there interceding for you. We have the Advocate — we do not have to go into despair.

This is a word that John likes — he uses it only twice: here and in chapter 4 verse 10. Look at it. Take a look at 1 John 4:10. He says, in this is love, not that we loved God, but that He loved us and sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins. So it is not like God is angry at you in heaven if you are a believer, and Jesus needs to come and say, no no it is going to be okay, do not be mad at them, here have this, have my blood so that you are not going to be mad at them. No. Out of love for his church, God the Father appointed Christ to be that propitiation — to be that person who is going to bring God and men together so that we can have fellowship with the Father.

This word is used in the Septuagint, the Greek translation of the Old Testament, several times. It is used for the Day of Atonement, for atonement in general, for forgiveness, for the sin offering. In all these things it pictures the Lord Jesus Christ. And it always has the idea of appeasing and covering, but it goes beyond that. It also includes the idea of bringing together — and that is what Jesus does for us. When we sin, he brings us together with the Father.

And notice that John says that Christ himself is the propitiation in verse 2. He himself is the propitiation — which is better than some other translations here. Remember in the Old Testament, what was necessary for the forgiveness of sin? The priest would bring the blood of a blameless animal before the Lord. It could not be the priest's own blood because he was not blameless. So it had to be the blood of a blameless animal, hoping that the Lord would forgive. Jesus is not only the priest, the Advocate who pleads for his people — he does that on the basis of his own blood. He is both the priest and the sacrifice. Hebrews 9:12 says, not with the blood of goats and calves, but with his own blood He entered the most holy place, once for all, having obtained eternal redemption.

The term propitiation is a related term to the word for mercy seat. You have heard of the mercy seat — it is the place in the most holy place where the high priest would once a year pour the blood of the innocent animal on behalf of the people of God. The place where the wrath of God was turned away. The presence of the Lord Jesus Christ is the place where the wrath of God is turned away. As Paul says in Ephesians 2:13, but now in Christ Jesus, you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ.

In Romans chapter 3 starting in the middle of verse 22, Paul uses a related term — he actually uses the term for the mercy seat itself, and says that Christ is that mercy seat. So Christ is not only the offering but also the place on which the offering is poured. He is everything. God did not leave anything for somebody else to do. Christ has accomplished it all.

*For there is no difference, for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, being justified freely by His grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus, whom God set forth as a propitiation by His blood, through faith, to demonstrate His righteousness, because in His forbearance God had passed over the sins that were previously committed, to demonstrate at the present time His righteousness, that He might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.*

So God himself justifies you. God himself declares you to be free from sin. God himself says, I look at my Son and I am seeing you; when I look at you I am seeing my Son.

## Application: The Same Obedience for Everyone

So John says, do not sin, work hard. But when you do sin, do not go into despair. The Lord Jesus Christ is before the Father interceding for you, on your behalf, by his own blood — an offering that Hebrews chapter 9 says the Father already received.

And notice that John says in verse 2 that Christ is the propitiation and not for ours only but also for the whole world. The phrase also for the whole world does not refer to some universal offer of salvation or some universal forgiveness of all people's sins. Rather it speaks to the uniqueness of the person and work of Christ. There is no other propitiation. If anyone in the world wants to come to the Father, they have to come to Jesus Christ. So in that sense he is the propitiation for the whole world. He is not for just these people and then these other people need to find another way. If anyone in the world wants to come to the Father, it has to be through Jesus Christ. He is unique. There is no other way to access the Father.

So John tells us, do not sin. Work hard at it. It feels like work because it is work. But it is work based on the grace of God. The Apostle Paul tells us that we are to work out our salvation with fear and trembling. The word for work there is the word spend energy — do the work. Why? Because it is God who is working in you to will and to do his good pleasure.

So how do you obey God as a single person? This way: by faith in the Lord Jesus Christ, knowing that he stands before the Father for you. And this faith results in working really hard to do what God calls you to do. And when you do not, you turn to Christ, you are forgiven, and you continue in obedience. It is simple. This does not mean it is easy, but it is simple.

Now how do you obey God as a married person? This way: by faith in the Lord Jesus Christ, knowing that he stands before the Father for you, this faith results in working really hard to do what God calls you to do. And when you do not, you turn to Christ, you are forgiven, and you continue in obedience.

Now how do you obey God in your old age? Have you fallen into a pattern? Yeah. Obedience is based on the same thing for everyone. Young people do not obey God differently than married people than old people than young people. It is all based on the work of God through Christ in us as his Spirit dwells in us. We do what the Bible says we do, and when we fail we turn to God and we are forgiven.

You cannot believe that, brothers and sisters, that no matter what we do as followers of Jesus Christ we are forgiven? We do not have to despair of that. And that thought actually frees us to obey. We do not have to be concerned about failing because even if we fail the Lord is going to forgive us. And we are free to follow him and to obey him and to risk for him because even when we fail he forgives us.

So my little children, do not sin. But if you do, you have an Advocate with the Father, the Lord Jesus Christ himself.

Let us pray together. Father, thank You that You are a God who forgives. We pray that the message of forgiveness would be an encouragement to Your people. We pray also that the message of obedience would be a convicting message to all of us, that we might grow in our holiness as we endeavor to follow You. We thank You that the Spirit of God is working in us. We thank You that Your Son intercedes for us. Strengthen us, Father, to follow You and to obey You and to turn to You when we fail in our obedience. We thank You for the Lord Jesus Christ and it is in His name that we pray. Amen.
